statement.markdown: Given a sorted array of integers, find the first and last occurrence of a given target value. If the target is not found, return [-1, -1]. The array is sorted in ascending order. The function should return the indices of the first and last occurrence of the target value in the array. If the target is not found, return [-1, -1].
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer n, which is the number of elements in the array. The second line contains n space-separated integers, which are the elements of the array. The third line contains an integer, which is the target value.
statement.outputFormat: The output should be two space-separated integers, which are the indices of the first and last occurrence of the target value in the array. If the target is not found, output -1 -1.

constraints.description: Number of elements in the array

examples.explanation: The first occurrence of 2 is at index 1 and the last occurrence is at index 2.
The target value 6 is not found in the array.

hints.text: You can use a two-pointer approach to find the first and last occurrence of a target value in a sorted array. Start by finding the first occurrence of the target value, then use a similar approach to find the last occurrence.

To find the first occurrence of the target value, use a two-pointer approach with the left pointer starting at the beginning of the array and the right pointer at the end. Move the pointers towards each other until they meet or the target value is found.

To find the last occurrence of the target value, use a similar two-pointer approach but with the left pointer starting at the end of the array and the right pointer at the beginning. Move the pointers towards each other until they meet or the target value is found.

When finding the first occurrence, if the left pointer is less than the right pointer and the middle element is equal to the target value, update the left pointer to the right of the middle element to continue searching for the first occurrence.

When finding the last occurrence, if the left pointer is less than the right pointer and the middle element is equal to the target value, update the right pointer to the left of the middle element to continue searching for the last occurrence.

editorial.title: First and Last Occurrence – A Binary Search Tale
editorial.intuition: When an array is sorted, equal values form a contiguous block. The problem boils down to locating the leftmost and rightmost indices of that block. A linear scan will find them but costs O(n); a binary search can exploit order to cut the search space in half each step, achieving O(log n).
editorial.bruteForce: A straightforward approach is to iterate through the array once, recording the first index where the element equals the target and the last index as you go. This works for any input but touches every element, so its time complexity is linear, O(n). While correct, it becomes sluggish when n reaches 10,000 or more, especially in tight time limits.
editorial.betterSolution: The key observation is that in a sorted array, all occurrences of the target are consecutive. Thus, the first occurrence is the smallest index i with nums[i] ≥ target, and the last is the largest index j with nums[j] ≤ target. Each of these can be found by a modified binary search that keeps track of the best candidate seen so far while narrowing the search interval. Performing two such searches yields the desired pair in logarithmic time.
editorial.optimalSolution: Implement two binary searches: one that moves the high pointer left when nums[mid] ≥ target, updating first when nums[mid] == target; another that moves the low pointer right when nums[mid] ≤ target, updating last when nums[mid] == target. Each search runs in O(log n) time and uses O(1) extra space, matching the stated optimal complexity.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: For the first search, whenever nums[mid] ≥ target we know that the first occurrence cannot be to the right of mid, so we set hi = mid – 1, preserving the invariant that the first occurrence lies within [lo,hi]. When nums[mid] == target we record mid as a candidate; any earlier occurrence must be to the left, so we continue searching left. This guarantees that when the loop ends, the recorded index is the smallest index with nums[i] == target. The second search is symmetric: when nums[mid] ≤ target we set lo = mid + 1, keeping the last occurrence within [lo,hi]; when nums[mid] == target we record mid as a candidate and continue searching right. Thus, the final recorded index is the largest index with nums[i] == target. Both invariants hold throughout, ensuring correctness.
editorial.dryRun: Consider nums=[1,2,2,2,3,4] and target=2. First search: mid=2 (value 2) → record first=2, hi=1. Next mid=0 (value 1) → lo=1. Next mid=1 (value 2) → record first=1, hi=0. Loop ends; first=1. Second search: mid=2 (value 2) → record last=2, lo=3. Next mid=4 (value 3) → hi=3. Next mid=3 (value 2) → record last=3, lo=4. Loop ends; last=3. Result [1,3].
commonMistakes: Assuming a single binary search can return both indices; it only finds one boundary unless modified.
Using the standard binary search that stops on equality and then scanning linearly to adjust, which reintroduces O(n) behavior.
Not updating the candidate index when nums[mid] equals target, thus missing earlier/later occurrences.
Swapping the conditions for moving lo and hi, breaking the invariant that the search interval always contains the target.
Using recursion instead of iteration and inadvertently creating O(log n) stack space, violating the O(1) space requirement.
Misinterpreting the problem as unsorted, leading to an O(n) linear scan instead of exploiting sorting.
editorial.timeComplexity: O(log n) for each binary search, overall O(log n)

functionSignature.java: public int[] solve(int[] nums, int target);
functionSignature.python: def solve(self, nums: list[int], target: int) -> list[int]:
